speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. Interview with Olga Kovacs. You were telling us about your schooling in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us about the classes you studied and the life at the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: The life at the school was convent life. We did not have uniforms. However, what they-- the nuns called 'les manches.' It was something like a half-sleeve that we had to put on our arms when we were in short sleeve blouses or sweaters. Otherwise, they-- they were-- they were only strict when it came to the lessons and the development of-- of the linguistic skills. That was the only thing that they really went after.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was the school specializing in languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was specializing in languages. They were all French nuns. And somebody told us that the school, Notre Dame de Sion, was founded by two converted Jews. I don't know whether this-- this is just sort of a hearsay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The old timers in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: The students or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The students. That they even knew one name-- Per Ratisbonne-- Pierre Ratisbonne. R-A-T-I-S-B-O-N-N-E. I never heard it, before or after. I never heard it denied or confirmed. But some of the old timers told us that was the case. Which is really beside the point because the people who were running the school were really marvelous. Very strict, but marvelous.

speaker: interviewer
text: What order were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Notre Dame de Sion. I don't know the order. They had the-- the black headgear, and then under it white sort of stiff something, and then they had a white collar. And of course black robes. And with rosary that they always had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were from France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's a good question. Because somebody told us that there was one sister, Soeur Praxede-- P-R-A-X-E-D-E-- who allegedly came from Czechoslovakia. I don't know. She had a marvelous accent, but it was not French accent. But the rest of the nuns were from France. We had to call them call 'mere,' mother. There was one thing I did not like. They expected you to kiss their hands. And I-- I was always forcing myself because I just-- I felt-- I felt I shouldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt it's humiliating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in a personal way or a religious way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably it has something-- something-- something psychological. Every single time when I kiss grandma, I wanted to kiss her hand and she withdrew it and she told me, my dear child, I am not a priest. You should never do that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the subconscious reason for it, probably. I really didn't go into it, you know, the philosophic for or against. Because as time went by, I had to accept it. But as often as I could, I avoided it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- do you know how many students were in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The classes were rather small because one-- and then also, we had various teachers. One teacher taught us English. She was a Hungarian woman who, I believe, finished her schooling in Britain because she spoke with a British accent. And then another nun taught us literature, then again another, like, history. About 10 or 12 students. It was a long refectory table. At the head sat the nun-- the head nun who was running the classes. And then we were sitting on the side. And we had homework to do. Written, and, of course, oral exam. Not exams, but they-- they-- they let you talk and you had to make some progress.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the language of instruction was French?

speaker: interviewee
text: French. When I-- when I walked into my first class, I didn't know one word in French. And I remember the mere told me, [SPEAKING FRENCH], which means, get it out, find your place, overcome. It means a lot of things in English when you say that. And they expected you to study French. And if you knew only two words, they expected you to use the two words and add up to the two in order to form a sentence. And that you should talk even if you didn't know the language. That's what they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was 1940 when you got to the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were how old? You were--

speaker: interviewer
text: 14. How did you feel when your parents told you that you had to go to this school? Were you-- What were your feelings at the time and how did you feel while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I did not have any negative feelings because somehow I felt that this will be a quiet environment for me. And I wasn't disappointed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wanted to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. I didn't want to stay in Salgotarjan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I-- I-- I-- I felt that-- first of all, I felt that I am not wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: By?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- by-- by the children, by the school system. Not that they told me, but they didn't have to tell me. I felt-- I felt a pressure. And I-- I felt the-- I even heard the unspoken words. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was rather difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there-- How many other Jewish children were there in the school? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Salgotarjan?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, in-- I'm sorry. In--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Notre Dame de Sion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a number. Well, just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably, as I-- as I recall, out of the 12 or 13, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about four or five Jewish girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: 12 Or 13 in the class or in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the class. In that particular class where I was. Because they graded you according to what you know. And if you didn't know enough in the first year, you had to repeat it. If you were good at the end, then you could jump a year. And actually you could go from first to the third grade if you-- if you you advanced that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you were living with friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you lived in their house and went to school every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes,

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any close friends at the school at the time? Did you become very close with any of the students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, especially with-- with one girl I-- I became good friends. But not-- not close friends, just good friends. My best friends became the daughter of the lady with whom I was staying with.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you in contact with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let-- letters. Letters and phone calls and they-- they-- they came to Budapest very often. Of course I could go home only on-- on the holidays, the school vacation. During the school vacation days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so did you have a-- the friends that you stayed with in Budapest, were they Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you continue to have a Jewish life with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not as observant as we were. But they were good Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you said in the school there was no pressure at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Religious pressure? You didn't have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't-- They didn't even ask us. No, absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The non-Jewish children, did they participate in-- in Catholic observance at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. They did-- As far as I know, and I might be off-- I never asked about this, so I'm not sure. But as far as I know, there were no religious teachings going on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like in Budapest for you, as a Jewish girl, at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my free days, most-- most of the time, I studied during the week. And then an uncle of my father lived in Budapest with his wife, [? Adner ?] and Margit Gelb-- G-E-L-B. And I spent a good deal of my free time with them. They were adorable people. They did not have children, and they were really doting on the cousins. And-- or the nieces, more precisely. And I spent some time with them. And I had private classes, because I wanted to advance my French [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] knowledge-- nonexistent knowledge. So I was very busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you feel any anti-Semitism in-- in Budapest at that time? You were there from 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as the war went on-- and by 1940, the Nazis invaded Russia-- I remember-- it was, I believe, in June--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940. In June of 1940 when the Nazis invaded Russia. Mother was staying at the hotel, visiting me, and we were just prepared to spend a few days in Budapest and then go home because the school year was over. And we came down from the hotel, and we noticed that they were painting certain signs on the sidewalk-- white signs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we found out why. Because they-- how do you say this in English? When they dimmed the lights, because they were afraid, probably, of air raids, because of the outbreak of the war. So that when you walk, you should see that sign-- For blackouts.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the blackouts. And that was that. I-- I-- I can see mother as we-- as we-- as we came downstairs, and saw the people doing this on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you-- did your family, your parents, tell you what was going on for them in Salgotarjan while you were in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, life was-- life was different, because eventually the business was taken away by anti-Jewish legislation sometime during the 1940s. And after the Nazi-Russian war broke out, they drafted men of a certain age, and my father had to go too. At that time, I believe there were no forced labor units yet. And eventually, they were released. For how long, and where, I don't remember, but it was within the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this would be your father and the other males in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other males in the family, yes. And that was probably between '40 and '42. I don't even remember for how long were they kept there. But they were within the country. Whereas gradually, probably after '42, they instituted the forced labor battalions. And they marched or shipped the men out to the Russian front.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Jewish men only? Or were there also--

speaker: interviewee
text: The forced labor units consisted of only Jews, as far as we know. And then all hell broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the period between 1940 and 1944 while you were living in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your personal life, you didn't really experience any hardships, you're saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and I can say that my life, during this time, was sheltered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had my family in Salgotarjan, a phone call away. Or when I get on the-- got on the train, it was a two and a half day-- two and a half hour journey.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I didn't feel threatened, and-- and nobody bothered me at school. And the Christian girls were nice. Even a few aristocrats who were there. You know in Hungary we had those barons and the counts and all this. And if they wanted to learn the language, then they went to a convent school. They were very decent people. Nobody bothered me. Did you have any idea what was going on in the rest of Europe at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gradually we have heard that something is happening in the East. We did not know what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Happening to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We heard about ghettos. But we never heard about the Einsatzgruppen. We never heard about the camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But one day, a neighbor's mail got mixed up with our mail. And it was a card, which we inadvertently read because it was in our mail.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably between '42 and '44. And it said-- it came from the Russian front, written by an officer-- we are system-- this is a direct quote-- 'We are systematically exterminating the Jews.' Unquote.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was on a postcard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Written by the officer to his family in Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, today we know what that means. It's-- it's a mobile killing unit. It's the Einsatzgruppen. It's-- with the aid of Hungarians and Ukrainians [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the rest of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS, mainly. We didn't know. We-- we-- we suspected that this is just some-- some local atrocity. We-- we-- we couldn't even imagine that this was the beginning of the final solution-- of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with this postcard? Did you show it to anyone, or talk about it to anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember, but the postcard was given or mailed-- I don't know how-- to the person to whom it belonged. How, and where, and by whom, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were home at the time this postcard arrived? Or you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard it from-- I heard it either from my aunt, [INAUDIBLE], or from my parents. I'm not sure anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you heard rumors but you had no concrete knowledge of anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so tell us what happened. What caused you to leave Budapest in '44? What happened at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 19th of March, 1944, a friend of mine called from-- I believe it was the Ministry of Foreign Affairs-- that the Nazis crossed the Hungarian border and they were approaching Budapest. That was an early morning call. I knew that the Berlin Express is leaving Budapest at 2:00 PM, and I could be on it if I pulled myself together. I immediately called my parents and, through certain masking metaphors, I let them know what's going on and that I am arriving in the afternoon. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I left, of course, everything I had at my-- at our friend's place. I only took my purse and the book. Got on the train way, and proceeded to the railway station. And somebody whom I found rather suspicious wanted to strike up a conversation. And by that time, I was suspecting the Gestapo and the SS and-- and-- and all-- all these evil people around us that they want to do me in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I jumped off, I took a cab, and rode to the station. I did not have any ID but one piece of paper that telled the world-- told the world that I am an Israelite, which is not the best ID in time of a Nazi invasion, of course. This was an official Hungarian document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, with the religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all Jews had that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. And if you were Christian, Roman Catholic. God forbid not to declare religion. That was Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I figured that, if I-- if I walk into a compartment where there are officers-- Hungarian officers, military officers-- probably I could get away with it. I didn't particularly look 'Jewish,' quote, unquote. And with no luggage but a book and a small purse, I was hoping for the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you left, did you speak to the sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just called and left a message that I'm going home on vacation. Nothing else. I'm sure they knew it. I mean, they-- they knew what was all about. And it-- it-- it was a trying journey because I was afraid any minute that somebody would come in and-- and would ask questions. Nobody bothered me. I got off, my parents were waiting for me, and I felt safe with them. 'Safe' in quotation mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did you decide-- you decided to go home because you thought it would be safer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided to go home because I did not expect anything good. And should anything bad happen, I should be with my family. I would bear an insurmountable guilt today had I not been part of the deportation with my parents. Had I not been-- had I been sp-- saved from the experience, it would be a horrendous feeling of guilt. And I don't feel survivor guilt because I stayed with them till the very end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone in Budapest tell you that you shouldn't go home before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to see my Uncle-- Uncle Bela, who was living in Budapest at the time, and eventually they-- they required-- they required-- or acquired, rather, Christian papers and they were hidden by somebody's chambermaid who was an angel and-- and did extraordinary things for them and for her own employers. And I told him. He said, well, it's up to you. And I-- I remember he gave me 40 forints just in case. And I said goodbye, and I left. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I felt that this-- I made a good decision.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like when you arrived in Salgotarjan?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time I arrived, of course, the invasion was a forgone con-- conclusion. I mean, it had a foregone conclusion. They were in Budapest. They had-- the good Hungarians prepared lists of every single Jew in every single town. So the Germans just to take the papers and take it from there. They were all apprised of everything, in every single town. The Hungarians started to collaborate with the Nazis even before the invasion. Unless they were warned that something will happ-- I don't know. I don't have any way to find this out. And I really don't care because I don't care about the Hungarians. I don't care about Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know that in your town-- these lists were given to them?

words.text: these lists were given to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had lists. They had lists, because eventually, the gendarmes-- and these were the most evil people you can imagine, the Hungarian gendarmes-- they were-- they were [? brigandists. ?] They were-- they were torturers. They were-- they were beasts. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I don't have any way of knowing whether there was a retribution after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these gendarmes, they had lists?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gendarmes picked up the people, eventually, who were suspected of having hidden something-- some valuable that belonged to them. They did not steal it. It belonged to them. And they subjected them to horrendous torture. They picked up my grandfather from the ghetto. My father, my uncle, and my aunt. And I cannot tell you what they did to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you to go back? We'll continue in a moment with this. Let me ask you to go back. And so, what were the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nazi act-- actions in Salgotarjan after you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gradually-- I believe it was in April-- there was a decree. We had to wear a yellow star sewn on this-- on the left side of the clothing. Without that, you couldn't go out. And it was-- in case it was not stitched properly, they could arrest you. And then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anything could have happened. In May-- sometimes in May, because I remember we were already in the ghetto on the day of my birthday. We had to go to the ghetto. They herded us in the ghetto. And we stayed there until the sixth of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this happen? How did they make you go to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a decree. There was a decree that in 24 hours, or 48 hours, you-- you have to-- you have to find a place because this part of town will become a ghetto. The other part of town will also be a ghetto. And you must go, or else. They didn't even say or else. You had to go. Only a few pieces of bedding, and a few pans and pots, and-- and just a few dresses or some-- some kind of a clothing we were able to collect and take to the ghetto. And we had to leave our apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your families-- Your rest of your family in Salgotarjan as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. We went-- We lived in the same-- same house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In one room, my grandparents and unc-- and the aunts with the children. And in the other room, I-- I-- I was with my parents, and then another aunt, and the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. In Salgotarjan, yeah. In May.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you all lived together in the same house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We-- we always tried to keep together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you pick-- how did you get to this particular house?

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular house belonged to a friend of my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the husband was, I think, already somewhere, I don't know where but-- because the woman was living alone. I think the husband was-- maybe he was er-- I don't-- I don't remember. Because by then, he must have been in his 50s. And she-- she-- she offered us the possi-- the courtesy to stay with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she was living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she was-- she was-- she-- Her house became the ghetto. Part of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, so what was life like for the period that you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: From bad to worse. After a few days, the gendarmes came in early morning and took away my father, my grandfather, my uncle, and my aunt to a nearby school. And they subjected them to horrendous torture. I was permitted to take food to my father. I never had a chance to see the other relatives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought I-- I just couldn't believe what I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did they take them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In order for them-- for the gendarmerie to get the information under torture from the people where they have hidden certain valuables.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a reason to suspect that your family had valuables?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my family was well-to-do. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And also, there were some people there who-- who shouldn't have been suspected of having this or that. But it was the Hungarian gendarmerie, and they were beasts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- How long were they held?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably about a week or less. I'm not sure. But by the time they came back, they were mentally, physically, and emotionally broken. My father, my uncle, my grandfather, had open wounds-- bleeding wounds-- on their heads. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lacerations ON all their bodies. They could hardly walk because of the beat-- the-- the-- severe beating to the sole of their feet. They pushed my aunt's head into a sack filled with horseradish, and they tied a knot around it. She just barely escaped. She's the aunt who-- who-- who came with us to Auschwitz.
